summary: Graham argues that programming languages become popular primarily because expert hackers like them, and that popularity in turn makes languages better through feedback and evolution. To win hackers, a language must be terse, hackable, available, and paired with real systems and strong libraries, not just elegant theory. He uses Lisp as a case study to argue that a new Lisp could succeed if it better serves what hackers actually want.
key_points: Expert hackers drive language adoption, and their preferences matter more than formal elegance or top-down mandates.
Popularity and quality reinforce each other through user feedback, so a language must gain traction to improve.
Hackers value brevity, hackability, and the ability to write quick throwaway programs that often grow into real systems.
A language must ship with a free implementation, good documentation, and a real system to hack.
A new Lisp could be successful if it focuses on powerful libraries and real-world hacking needs.
money_quotes: Programming languages are not theorems. They're tools, designed for people, and they have to be designed to suit human strengths and weaknesses as much as shoes have to be designed for human feet.
Given an initial critical mass and enough time, a programming language probably becomes about as popular as it deserves to be.

A really good language should be both clean and dirty: cleanly designed, with a small core of well understood and highly orthogonal operators, but dirty in the sense that it lets hackers have their way with it.
If you want to make a language that is used for big systems, you have to make it good for writing throwaway programs, because that's where big systems come from.
The most important part of design is redesign.
tone: analytical and reflective, with a pragmatic, opinionated edge
key_insight: A language wins by serving hackers’ practical needs—brevity, hackability, libraries, and a real system to hack—because their adoption drives both popularity and long-term quality.
